meta.title: AI Engineering
meta.subtitle: ship LLM & agent systems
meta.blurb: The applied LLM stack — pre-training, RAG, agents, reasoning, and multimodal generation.

t-llm-overview.label: LLM Overview & Foundations
t-llm-overview.desc: What large language models are, how next-token prediction works, and why scale plus data turned them into general-purpose engines.

links.title: Karpathy — Neural Networks: Zero to Hero

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An LLM predicts the **next token** given the ones before it. Trained on enormous text, that single objective yields models that write, reason, and code.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: They're built on the **Transformer**, and quality scales with data, parameters, and compute (scaling laws). A raw base model is just a predictor — usefulness comes from post-training.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: By mid-2026 the frontier is crowded — **GPT-5.5**, **Claude Opus 4.7 / Sonnet 5**, **Gemini 3.5 Flash**, and **Grok 4.3** on the closed side; **DeepSeek V4**, **Llama 4 Scout/Maverick** (up to 10 M context), **Qwen 3.6**, **Mistral Large 3**, **GLM-5**, and **Kimi K2** on the open side. They differ mostly in data mix, tokenizer, attention variants, and training recipe — not the core idea — and open-weight models now match closed leaders on many tasks.

t-pretraining.label: Pre-Training
t-pretraining.desc: Teaching a base model language and world knowledge by predicting tokens across a massive, cleaned corpus.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Feed the model trillions of tokens and train it to predict the next one. This is where it absorbs grammar, facts, and patterns.

levels.label: The pipeline
levels.body: **Collect** web data (Common Crawl) → **clean & filter** (FineWeb, Dolma, RefinedWeb) → **tokenize** (BPE) → train a Transformer.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Data quality now matters more than raw size — most gains come from filtering and dedup. It's expensive, so you'll usually build on someone else's base model.

t-posttraining.label: Post-Training
t-posttraining.desc: Turning a raw predictor into a helpful, safe assistant via supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from feedback.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A base model just continues text. Post-training teaches it to follow instructions and act like an assistant.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SFT** on example conversations, then **preference alignment** — a reward model scores answers and RL (PPO or **GRPO**) optimises for them. **DPO** skips the reward model entirely; **RLAIF** replaces humans with an AI judge. For checkable tasks, **verifiable rewards (RLVR)** replace the human.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: This stage shapes helpfulness, tone, and safety far more than pre-training. **GRPO** (DeepSeek's group-relative approach) achieves alignment with far less compute than PPO. Reward design, preference data quality, and overfitting to the reward model are the hard parts.

t-llm-eval.label: Evaluation
t-llm-eval.desc: Measuring model quality with automatic metrics, task benchmarks, and human judgement.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: You can't improve what you don't measure. Evaluation asks one question: is the output actually **good** — accurate, helpful, and safe?

levels.label: Three approaches
levels.body: **Automatic metrics** (perplexity, BLEU/ROUGE) are cheap but weak; **task benchmarks** (MMLU-Pro, GPQA Diamond, SWE-Bench Verified) probe specific skills; **human / LLM-as-judge** preference comparisons (arena Elo) capture open-ended quality.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: By 2026 classic suites like **MMLU are saturated**, so the field leans on **SWE-Bench Verified**, **GPQA Diamond**, **BFCL** (function-calling), **Aider Polyglot** (coding), and **Chatbot Arena Elo**. Static benchmarks all suffer some contamination — the real win is a small eval set for *your* task, plus arena-style blind preference.

t-chatbot-design.label: Chatbot System Design
t-chatbot-design.desc: Assembling models, prompts, memory, and guardrails into a working chatbot.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A chatbot is far more than one model call. It's a **system**: a model, a system prompt, conversation **memory**, and **guardrails** wired together.

levels.label: The pieces
levels.body: Manage the **context window** (history + retrieved docs + tool results), add **memory** for long sessions, and put **guardrails** on inputs and outputs — moderation, schema validation, refusal handling.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Production concerns dominate: **streaming**, **latency/cost** budgets, **prompt caching**, model fallbacks, observability (OpenTelemetry-aligned tracing), and evals. Context windows now reach 1–2 M tokens, but managing them well — not just stuffing them — is still the work.

t-adaptation.label: Adaptation Techniques
t-adaptation.desc: Ways to make a general model fit your task — prompting, retrieval, and fine-tuning, cheapest to most involved.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: You rarely train from scratch. Adapt a pretrained model: prompt it, give it retrieved context, or fine-tune it.

levels.label: Rule of thumb
levels.body: Try **prompt engineering** first, add **RAG** for fresh or proprietary knowledge, and **fine-tune** only when behaviour or format must change.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most production systems are **layered**: a good prompt, retrieval for fresh/proprietary knowledge, and light fine-tuning only where behaviour must change. Start cheap, add complexity only when your evals demand it.

t-finetuning.label: Fine-tuning
t-finetuning.desc: Updating a model's weights on your data — efficiently, without retraining everything.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Fine-tuning **updates the model's weights** on your data. Reach for it only when prompting and RAG can't get the behaviour or format you need.

levels.label: Do it efficiently
levels.body: Full fine-tuning is expensive, so use **PEFT**. Methods like **LoRA / QLoRA** train a few small matrices and leave the base frozen — cheap, fast, and swappable per task.

levels.label: When to reach for it
levels.body: Fine-tune to change **style, format, or a narrow skill**; use **RAG** to add knowledge. They compose — **RAFT** even fine-tunes a model to use retrieved context well. Watch for **catastrophic forgetting**.

t-prompt-eng.label: Prompt Engineering
t-prompt-eng.desc: Steering a frozen model with well-designed inputs — the fastest, cheapest lever.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Change behaviour just by changing the prompt — no training needed.

levels.label: Techniques
levels.body: **Zero/few-shot** examples, **chain-of-thought** ('think step by step'), and role/context framing.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Cheap to iterate but brittle and model-specific; for reliability, combine with retrieval, tools, and evaluation.

t-rag.label: RAG Overview
t-rag.desc: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — fetch relevant documents and feed them to the model so answers are grounded and current.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Instead of relying on what the model memorised, retrieve relevant text and let it answer from that.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Two stages: **retrieval** (find the right chunks) and **generation** (answer using them). Fixes stale knowledge and cuts hallucination.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most production 'AI on your data' systems are RAG. The quality lives in chunking, embeddings, and retrieval — not the LLM.

t-retrieval.label: Retrieval
t-retrieval.desc: Finding the most relevant chunks for a query — parsing, chunking, indexing, and embedding.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Retrieval finds the **most relevant chunks** for a query so the model answers from them instead of guessing.

levels.label: The pipeline
levels.body: **Parse** documents → **chunk** them → **index** (keyword, vector, or graph) → **embed** queries and chunks so semantic similarity becomes distance.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Quality lives here, not in the LLM. **Hybrid** search (BM25 + vectors, fused with reciprocal-rank fusion) plus a **reranker** beats pure vector search on most corpora. Agentic RAG — multi-step retrieval with reasoning — is gaining ground for complex queries.

t-generation.label: Generation
t-generation.desc: Producing the grounded answer — searching the index and prompting the model with retrieved context.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Generation is the second half of RAG: take the retrieved chunks and **prompt the model to answer from them**.

levels.label: How
levels.body: Run **nearest-neighbour search** for the top-k chunks, then format them into the prompt with instructions to **ground** the answer and **cite** sources.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Guard against **context stuffing** (more isn't better), the **lost-in-the-middle** ordering effect, and ungrounded answers. Ask for citations, then verify them.

t-rag-eval.label: RAG Evaluation
t-rag-eval.desc: Judging a RAG system on whether it retrieved the right context and answered faithfully.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A RAG system can fail in two places: **retrieval** (wrong documents) or **generation** (ignores the right ones). Evaluation has to separate the two.

levels.label: What to measure
levels.body: **Context relevance** (did we fetch the right chunks?), **faithfulness** (is the answer grounded in them?), and **answer correctness** (is it actually right?).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Tools like **RAGAS** and LLM-as-judge automate this. A faithful answer built on bad context is still wrong — fix retrieval first.

t-agents-overview.label: Agents Overview
t-agents-overview.desc: What separates an agent from a plain LLM call: planning, tool use, and acting over multiple steps.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An agent is an LLM that takes **actions** (call tools, search, run code) in a loop — not just emitting text once.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: A spectrum of autonomy: fixed **workflows** (you wire the steps) → **multi-step agents** (the model decides the steps). Frameworks like **LangGraph**, **Google ADK**, and **OpenAI Agents SDK** encode these patterns.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: More autonomy means more capability but less predictability. Use the least autonomy that solves the task. By mid-2026, production agents combine reasoning models, tool-calling, and guardrails — **OpenAI Agents SDK**, **Google ADK**, **LangGraph**, and **AWS Strands** are the leading frameworks. The hard part is reliability, not capability — most agent failures are missing guardrails, not a weak model.

t-workflows.label: Workflows
t-workflows.desc: Predefined multi-step patterns that orchestrate LLM calls reliably.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **workflow** wires LLM calls together along a path *you* design — predictable and debuggable, unlike a free-roaming agent.

levels.label: Common patterns
levels.body: **Prompt chaining** (step by step), **routing** (classify then dispatch), **parallelization** (fan-out and vote), **orchestrator-worker**, and **reflection** (critique then revise).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Anthropic's guidance: use the **simplest** workflow that works, and add agentic autonomy only when the task truly needs it. Model the steps as an explicit **state graph** (LangGraph, Google ADK) for resumability and observability.

t-tools.label: Tools
t-tools.desc: Giving the model real capabilities — functions, APIs, and external systems it can invoke.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Tools let the model *do* things — search the web, run code, query a database — by calling functions you expose.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: The model emits a structured **tool call**; your runtime executes it and feeds the result back. Schema formatting and reliable execution matter.

levels.label: MCP
levels.body: **MCP** (Model Context Protocol) standardises how models connect to tools and data sources, so integrations are reusable across apps. By mid-2026, MCP has broad IDE and framework support (Cursor, Kiro, Claude Desktop, Copilot), making it the de facto standard for tool-to-model communication. **A2A** handles agent-to-agent discovery and delegation.

t-multistep.label: Multi-Step Agents
t-multistep.desc: Agents that plan and iterate autonomously toward a goal.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A multi-step agent **decides its own steps**. It loops — think, act with a tool, observe — until the goal is met.

levels.label: Patterns
levels.body: **ReAct** interleaves reasoning and acting; **Reflexion / ReWOO** add self-critique and decoupled planning; **tree search** explores several action paths.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Autonomy is powerful and risky. Add **step limits**, **budgets**, **human-in-the-loop** checkpoints, and tracing. Most 'agent' failures are missing guardrails, not a weak model.

t-multiagent.label: Multi-Agent Systems
t-multiagent.desc: Multiple cooperating agents — their use-cases, coordination challenges, and protocols like A2A and AG-UI.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Instead of one agent, use **several specialised agents** that cooperate — say a researcher, a coder, and a reviewer.

levels.label: How they coordinate
levels.body: A coordinator delegates subtasks, or agents talk peer-to-peer. Standards like **A2A** (Agent-to-Agent) let agents from different vendors discover and message one another; **AG-UI** standardises agent-to-user streaming interaction.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: More agents means more coordination overhead, cost, and failure modes. Reach for multi-agent only when a single tool-using agent clearly can't divide and conquer the task. Protocols (**A2A**, **AG-UI**) and frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, **OpenAI Agents SDK**) are converging but still evolving. In 2026, multi-agent systems power deep research, complex code generation, and autonomous enterprise workflows — but reliability requires extensive testing and observability.

t-agent-eval.label: Agent Evaluation
t-agent-eval.desc: Assessing agents on task success, efficiency, and reliability across multi-step runs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Evaluating an agent is harder than scoring one answer — you're judging a whole **multi-step trajectory**.

levels.label: What to measure
levels.body: **Task success** (did it finish?), **efficiency** (steps, tokens, cost, latency), and **reliability** (does it succeed repeatably?). Trace every tool call.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use end-to-end task benchmarks (**SWE-Bench Verified**, **BFCL** for tool-calling) plus per-step checks. Non-determinism means many runs and pass@k-style metrics, not a single score. Observability (tracing every tool call and decision) is essential.

t-reasoning-models.label: Reasoning & Thinking LLMs
t-reasoning-models.desc: Models trained to 'think' before answering — spending extra compute on internal reasoning for hard problems.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Reasoning models — OpenAI's **o3/o4-mini**, **DeepSeek-R1**, **Claude** (extended thinking), and **Gemini** (Deep Think) — generate a long internal chain of thought before the final answer.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: They trade latency and compute for accuracy on math, code, and logic — 'thinking longer' genuinely helps. They cost 4–17× more per query and are 5–60× slower, but meaningfully outperform on complex tasks.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The ability comes from training on reasoning traces and RL with verifiers, plus inference-time techniques that scale test-time compute. By 2026 reasoning is no longer a separate model class — it's a mode built into frontier models (thinking budgets you can dial).

t-inference-time.label: Inference-time Techniques
t-inference-time.desc: Getting better answers by spending more compute at generation time — no retraining.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Spend **more compute at generation time** — no retraining — to get better answers on hard problems.

levels.label: The levers
levels.body: **Longer chains of thought**, **parallel sampling** (generate many, vote), **sequential refinement**, **tree-of-thoughts** search, and **verifier-guided** selection.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: This is the **test-time scaling** behind reasoning models: accuracy climbs with the thinking budget — but so do latency and cost, so tune it to the difficulty of each query.

t-training-time.label: Training-time Techniques
t-training-time.desc: Baking reasoning ability into the model's weights.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Instead of paying every inference, **bake reasoning into the weights** so the model thinks well by default.

levels.label: How
levels.body: **SFT on verified reasoning traces** (STaR), **RL with a verifier** (RLVR — reward correct answers on checkable tasks), and **reward models** scoring whole outcomes (ORM) or each step (PRM). **GRPO** groups completions and computes relative advantage without a separate critic.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: **DeepSeek-R1** showed that pure RL with verifiable rewards can induce strong reasoning without human-labelled steps — the 2025 unlock that 2026 models build on. GRPO and DAPO are now the go-to training recipes.

t-local-deploy.label: Local Deployment
t-local-deploy.desc: Running open/reasoning models on your own hardware — quantization, inference servers, and trade-offs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Run **open-weight models on your own hardware** — for privacy, cost control, offline use, or simply to learn how it works.

levels.label: How
levels.body: **Quantize** the weights (4-bit GGUF / AWQ) to shrink memory, then serve with **llama.cpp** or **Ollama** on a laptop, or **vLLM** / **SGLang** for multi-user GPU throughput. Apple users get native **MLX** performance.

levels.label: Trade-offs
levels.body: You trade peak quality and ops simplicity for **control, privacy, and zero per-token cost**. 4-bit quantization is usually near-lossless; sizing VRAM to the model is the gotcha. vLLM's PagedAttention handles concurrency; llama.cpp excels at single-stream on CPU/edge.

t-genmodels.label: Generative Models Overview
t-genmodels.desc: The model families behind image and video generation, and how they differ.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Several ways to generate images: **VAEs**, **GANs**, autoregressive models, and — dominant today — **diffusion**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Diffusion learns to **denoise** random noise into an image step by step, conditioned on text. Modern systems work in a compressed **latent space** for efficiency.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Each family trades off quality, diversity, and training stability. Diffusion won images, with **Diffusion Transformers (DiT)** replacing U-Nets as the backbone of choice for both image and video generation in 2025–2026.

t-t2i.label: Text-to-Image
t-t2i.desc: Generating images from text prompts with diffusion.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Generate an image from a text prompt by **denoising** random noise into a picture, guided by the words.

levels.label: How
levels.body: A **U-Net or DiT** backbone learns to reverse a noising process; **classifier-free guidance** steers it toward the prompt; a text encoder (CLIP / T5) supplies the conditioning. By 2026 **DiT** has largely replaced U-Net at the frontier.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Fewer sampling steps is faster, more is sharper. Evaluate with **FID** (quality and diversity) and **CLIP score** (text alignment). Latent diffusion makes it cheap enough to run locally. Leading systems include **DALL·E 3**, **Midjourney**, **Stable Diffusion 3.5**, **FLUX**, and **Ideogram**.

t-t2v.label: Text-to-Video
t-t2v.desc: Extending diffusion to video — adding the time dimension, large-scale compute, and native audio.

links.title: Sora — Video as a World Simulator

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Text-to-video adds the **time dimension** to diffusion — generate frames that are coherent *and* consistent across time, now with native audio.

levels.label: How
levels.body: Run diffusion in a compressed **latent space** (LDM) with **spatiotemporal DiT** blocks, trained on huge, heavily-filtered video-caption datasets. By 2026 clips reach 8–20 seconds at up to 4K resolution.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The hard parts are **compute, memory, and temporal consistency**. Leaders include **Google Veo 3.1**, **Kling v3**, **Seedance 2.0**, **Sora 2**, and **Runway Gen-4**. Open-weight alternatives (**Wan**, **LTX-Video**, **HunyuanVideo**) enable self-hosted pipelines.

t-slm-training.label: SLMs & Efficient Training
t-slm-training.desc: Building and training Small Language Models — compact models you can fine-tune and run locally for narrow tasks, at a fraction of the cost.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Bigger isn't always better. **Small Language Models (SLMs)** — roughly under 10 B parameters (Phi-4 mini, Gemma 3n, Qwen 3.5 small, Llama 3.2 1B–8B, SmolLM2) — can match large models on narrow tasks while running on a laptop, phone, or edge device with **no network**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Train or fine-tune them efficiently with **JAX** (XLA-compiled for fast iteration) or PyTorch, plus **distillation from reasoning models**, **LoRA/QLoRA**, and synthetic data. SLMs are particularly strong for **tool-calling, function calling, constrained RAG, and speculative decoding** (drafting tokens for a larger verifier) at a fraction of the cost.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The trade-off is raw capability vs **cost, latency, and privacy**. SLMs win for on-device, high-volume, or privacy-sensitive workloads. Quantized (4-bit GGUF) models fit 8 GB RAM with near-lossless quality. Key families in mid-2026: Microsoft **Phi-4 mini**, Google **Gemma 3n** (on-device optimised), Alibaba **Qwen 3.5 small**, Meta **Llama 3.2** (1B–8B), and Hugging Face **SmolLM2**. Distillation from frontier reasoning models is the dominant recipe for punching above weight class.

l-data-collection.label: Data Collection
l-data-collection.desc: Gathering raw text at scale — crawling the web yourself or using public dumps like Common Crawl.

l-data-cleaning.label: Data Cleaning
l-data-cleaning.desc: Filtering, deduplicating, and quality-scoring raw text. Curated sets (FineWeb, Dolma, RefinedWeb) show how much this matters.

l-tokenization.label: Tokenization
l-tokenization.desc: Splitting text into sub-word units the model consumes; Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is the common scheme.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **BPE** starts from bytes and repeatedly merges the most frequent pair, building a vocab of ~30k–130k sub-words. Sub-words beat whole words (no out-of-vocab) and beat characters (shorter sequences).

l-architecture.label: Architecture
l-architecture.desc: The Transformer and its variants (GPT, DeepSeek, Qwen, Gemma) — attention layers letting each token attend to the rest.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Self-attention lets every token look at every other — but it's **O(n²)** in sequence length. Modern decoder-only stacks add **RoPE** positions and **grouped-query / multi-head-latent attention** to keep long context affordable.

l-text-generation.label: Text Generation
l-text-generation.desc: Turning probabilities into text: greedy/beam search and sampling with top-k / top-p (nucleus) to trade off quality and diversity.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Temperature** scales the logits (low = focused, high = creative); **top-p / nucleus** keeps the smallest set of tokens summing to p (e.g. 0.9); **greedy** is deterministic but bland.

l-sft.label: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
l-sft.desc: Fine-tuning the base model on curated instruction/response pairs so it learns the assistant format.

l-rlhf.label: RL & RLHF
l-rlhf.desc: Optimising responses against a learned reward model with RL (PPO); verifiable rewards replace human raters for checkable tasks.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Train a **reward model** from human preference pairs, then optimise the policy with **PPO**. **DPO** skips the separate reward model by optimising the preference objective directly — simpler and now very common.

l-trad-metrics.label: Traditional Metrics
l-trad-metrics.desc: Automatic scores (perplexity, BLEU/ROUGE) — cheap but weak proxies for real quality.

l-benchmarks.label: Task Benchmarks
l-benchmarks.desc: Standardised suites (MMLU-style) that probe specific capabilities.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Multiple-choice suites like **MMLU** are largely saturated by 2026. Capability now gets measured on **SWE-Bench Verified**, **GPQA Diamond**, **Terminal-Bench**, and **FrontierMath** — harder, less gameable tasks.

l-human-eval.label: Human Eval & Leaderboards
l-human-eval.desc: Human preference comparisons and arena leaderboards — the closest thing to ground truth for open-ended output.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Show two answers blind, collect the preferred one, and fit an **Elo / Bradley-Terry** rating across many votes. **LMArena** is the canonical public leaderboard built this way.

l-peft.label: PEFT
l-peft.desc: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning — train a few extra weights instead of the whole model.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning trains a tiny fraction of weights and matches full fine-tuning on many tasks — far less memory, and you can keep many small task adapters around one shared base.

l-lora.label: Adapters & LoRA
l-lora.desc: Inject small low-rank matrices into layers and train only those. Cheap, fast, swappable per task.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Freeze the weight **W** and learn a low-rank update **B·A** with rank r ≈ 8–64, training well under 1% of parameters. **QLoRA** stacks LoRA on a 4-bit base, so you can fine-tune a 70B model on a single GPU.

l-raft.label: RAFT
l-raft.desc: Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning — train the model to use (and ignore distractor) retrieved documents.

l-fewshot.label: Few-shot / Zero-shot
l-fewshot.desc: Showing zero or a handful of examples in the prompt to demonstrate the task.

l-cot.label: Chain-of-Thought
l-cot.desc: Prompting the model to reason in steps before answering, improving multi-step accuracy.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Adding 'think step by step' surfaces intermediate reasoning. Gains are biggest on **multi-step math and logic**, and it pairs naturally with **self-consistency** — sample several chains and take the majority answer.

l-role-prompt.label: Role & Context Prompting
l-role-prompt.desc: Assigning a persona and injecting user-specific context to shape responses.

l-doc-parsing.label: Document Parsing
l-doc-parsing.desc: Extracting clean text and structure from PDFs, HTML, etc. — rule-based or AI-based.

l-chunking.label: Chunking
l-chunking.desc: Splitting documents into retrievable pieces; size and overlap strongly affect quality.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A common start is **256–1024 tokens** per chunk with **10–20% overlap**. Recursive or semantic splitting that respects headings and sentences beats naive fixed-size cuts on structured documents.

l-indexing.label: Indexing
l-indexing.desc: Building searchable indexes — keyword, full-text, knowledge-graph, or vector-based.

l-embeddings.label: Embeddings
l-embeddings.desc: Embedding models map text to vectors so semantic similarity becomes distance.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An embedding model maps text to a vector so **cosine similarity ≈ semantic similarity**. Pick one off the **MTEB** leaderboard and weigh dimension (and therefore index size/cost) against quality.

l-ann-search.label: Nearest-Neighbor Search
l-ann-search.desc: Finding similar vectors — exact, or approximate (ANN) for speed at scale.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Exact k-NN is fine at small scale; past that, **HNSW** builds a navigable small-world graph that trades a little recall for big speed-ups. It's what most vector databases use under the hood.

l-rag-prompting.label: Prompting for RAG
l-rag-prompting.desc: Structuring retrieved context into the prompt so the model stays grounded and cites it.

l-context-relevance.label: Context Relevance
l-context-relevance.desc: Did retrieval surface the documents that actually matter?

l-faithfulness.label: Faithfulness
l-faithfulness.desc: Is the answer supported by the retrieved context, not invented?

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Check that **each claim in the answer is entailed by the retrieved context** — typically with an LLM-as-judge or an NLI model. A fluent answer that isn't grounded is a hallucination, however confident.

l-answer-correctness.label: Answer Correctness
l-answer-correctness.desc: Is the final answer actually right for the user's question?

l-agent-vs.label: Agents vs Agentic Systems vs LLMs
l-agent-vs.desc: A raw LLM responds; an agentic system adds tools/memory/control flow; a full agent decides its own steps.

l-agency-levels.label: Agency Levels
l-agency-levels.desc: From scripted workflows to autonomous multi-step agents — how much the model is trusted to decide.

l-prompt-chaining.label: Prompt Chaining
l-prompt-chaining.desc: Feed one call's output into the next — decompose a task into ordered steps.

l-routing.label: Routing
l-routing.desc: Classify the input and send it to the right specialised prompt or model.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A cheap classifier (or small model) inspects each request and sends it to the right model or prompt — route easy queries to a small model and hard ones to a frontier model to cut cost and latency.

l-parallelization.label: Parallelization
l-parallelization.desc: Run subtasks at once (sectioning) or sample multiple answers and vote.

l-reflection.label: Reflection
l-reflection.desc: Have the model critique and revise its own output before finalising.

l-orchestrator.label: Orchestrator-Worker
l-orchestrator.desc: A coordinator LLM delegates subtasks to worker calls and assembles results.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A planner LLM breaks the task into subtasks, spawns worker calls, and merges their results. Use it when the subtasks **aren't known up front** — unlike a fixed prompt chain.

l-tool-calling.label: Tool Calling
l-tool-calling.desc: The model requests a function with arguments; the app runs it and returns the result.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The model returns a **structured JSON call** naming a function and its arguments; your code executes it and feeds the result back. Repeat the loop until the model answers without a call.

l-tool-formatting.label: Tool Formatting
l-tool-formatting.desc: Describing tools with schemas so the model knows what it can call and how.

l-tool-execution.label: Tool Execution
l-tool-execution.desc: Safely running the requested action and handling errors and timeouts.

l-mcp.label: MCP
l-mcp.desc: Model Context Protocol — an open standard for connecting models to tools and data in a reusable way.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **MCP** is a client-server standard: a server exposes **tools, resources, and prompts**, and any MCP-aware app (Claude Desktop, IDEs, agents) can use them. Write one integration, reuse it everywhere.

l-planning.label: Planning Autonomy
l-planning.desc: Letting the model decide the sequence of actions instead of hard-coding it.

l-react.label: ReAct
l-react.desc: Interleave reasoning and acting — think, act, observe, repeat.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **ReAct** runs a loop: the model emits a *Thought*, then an *Action* (a tool call), your runtime returns an *Observation*, and it repeats until done — reasoning and acting interleaved.

l-reflexion.label: Reflexion / ReWOO
l-reflexion.desc: Add self-reflection and decoupled planning to improve across attempts.

l-tree-search.label: Tree Search for Agents
l-tree-search.desc: Explore multiple action paths and pick the best, like search over plans.

l-a2a.label: A2A Protocol
l-a2a.desc: Agent-to-Agent communication standards that let separate agents coordinate.

l-inference-scaling.label: Inference-time Scaling
l-inference-scaling.desc: Spend more compute per query (longer reasoning, more samples) to raise accuracy.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: More **test-time compute** — longer chains of thought, more samples, or explicit search — raises accuracy with no retraining. This is the core insight behind the o-series and other reasoning models.

l-cot-prompt.label: CoT Prompting
l-cot-prompt.desc: Elicit step-by-step reasoning at inference to improve hard tasks.

l-parallel-sampling.label: Parallel Sampling
l-parallel-sampling.desc: Generate many candidate answers at once and select among them.

l-sequential-sampling.label: Sequential Sampling
l-sequential-sampling.desc: Iteratively refine an answer across successive attempts.

l-tot.label: Tree of Thoughts
l-tot.desc: Branch and explore multiple reasoning paths, then pick the best.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Branch into several reasoning paths, score the partial states, and **search** (BFS/DFS) over them — beating a single linear chain on puzzles and planning problems.

l-verifier-search.label: Search Against a Verifier
l-verifier-search.desc: Use a checker to score candidates and guide the search.

l-star.label: SFT on Reasoning Data (STaR)
l-star.desc: Fine-tune on generated, verified reasoning traces to teach step-by-step solving.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Generate solutions, **keep only those with the correct final answer**, and fine-tune on those traces. The model bootstraps its own reasoning data — no human step-by-step labels needed.

l-rl-verifier.label: RL with a Verifier
l-rl-verifier.desc: Reward correct final answers on checkable tasks and let RL improve reasoning.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: On checkable tasks (math, code, unit tests) the **verifier is the reward signal** — no human labels. Pure RL against verifiers is what produced DeepSeek-R1's reasoning.

l-reward-modeling.label: Reward Modeling (ORM/PRM)
l-reward-modeling.desc: Score whole outcomes (ORM) or each reasoning step (PRM) to guide training.

l-self-refine.label: Self-Refinement
l-self-refine.desc: Train the model to critique and improve its own answers.

l-meta-cot.label: Internalizing Search (Meta-CoT)
l-meta-cot.desc: Teach the model to perform search-like reasoning internally.

l-vae.label: VAE
l-vae.desc: Variational Autoencoders — encode to a latent distribution and decode; the basis for latent diffusion.

l-gans.label: GANs
l-gans.desc: Generator vs discriminator adversarial training; sharp images but unstable.

l-autoregressive.label: Auto-regressive Models
l-autoregressive.desc: Generate pixels/tokens one at a time, like language models for images.

l-diffusion.label: Diffusion Models
l-diffusion.desc: Iteratively denoise from noise to image; current state of the art.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: A **forward** process adds Gaussian noise over many steps; the model learns the **reverse**, denoising one step at a time. Samplers like **DDIM** cut the step count, trading a little quality for speed.

l-t2i-data.label: Data Preparation
l-t2i-data.desc: Curating image-text pairs and captions for training.

l-diffusion-arch.label: Diffusion Architectures
l-diffusion-arch.desc: U-Net and Diffusion Transformers (DiT) as the denoising backbone.

l-diffusion-training.label: Diffusion Training
l-diffusion-training.desc: A forward process adds noise; the model learns the reverse, denoising process.

l-diffusion-sampling.label: Diffusion Sampling
l-diffusion-sampling.desc: Iterative denoising at inference; fewer steps is faster, more steps is higher quality.

l-t2i-eval.label: Evaluation
l-t2i-eval.desc: Image quality and diversity plus text alignment — IS, FID, and CLIP score.

l-ldm.label: Latent Diffusion (LDM)
l-ldm.desc: Run diffusion in a compressed latent space for efficiency; compression networks shrink the data.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Run diffusion inside a compressed **VAE latent** instead of raw pixels — roughly an 8× cut in compute and memory. This is the trick that made **Stable Diffusion** runnable on consumer GPUs.

l-t2v-data.label: Data Preparation
l-t2v-data.desc: Filtering, standardising, and caching video latents at scale.

l-dit-video.label: DiT for Video
l-dit-video.desc: Diffusion Transformer architectures adapted to spatiotemporal data.

l-t2v-scaling.label: Large-Scale Training
l-t2v-scaling.desc: The compute, memory, and pipeline challenges of video models.

l-t2v-system.label: T2V System Design
l-t2v-system.desc: Putting the full text-to-video pipeline together.

links.title: Sora — Video as a World Simulator

l-jax-compilation.label: JAX & XLA
l-jax-compilation.desc: Using JAX — which compiles array code to the XLA compiler — for high-performance, fast-iterating training of smaller models.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **JAX** JIT-compiles array code through **XLA**, with `vmap`/`pmap` for automatic vectorisation and multi-device parallelism — especially strong on **TPUs** and tight research iteration loops.

l-slm-distillation.label: Knowledge Distillation
l-slm-distillation.desc: Training a small 'student' model to mimic a larger 'teacher', transferring capability into a compact, deployable model.

links.title: Distilling the Knowledge in a NN

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Train a small **student** on the large **teacher's** soft probability outputs (with a temperature), transferring much of the capability into a model small enough to deploy on a laptop or edge device.

l-deterministic-retrieval.label: Deterministic / Lexical Retrieval
l-deterministic-retrieval.desc: Embedding-free, exact-match retrieval — BM25 and SQLite FTS5 full-text search — cheaper and more predictable than pure vector search, and often combined with it (hybrid).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **BM25** scores by term frequency with document-length normalisation — embedding-free and predictable. In practice, **hybrid** (BM25 + vectors fused with reciprocal-rank fusion) usually beats either alone.

l-graph-ast-traversal.label: Graph & AST Traversal
l-graph-ast-traversal.desc: Parsing Abstract Syntax Trees and codebase dependency graphs to give an assistant structural, relationship-aware context — not just text similarity.

l-persistent-memory.label: Persistent Cross-Project Memory
l-persistent-memory.desc: Attaching a local store (e.g. SQLite with sqlite-vec) to an MCP server so coding agents keep continuous, cross-session state and recall.

l-agentcore-strands.label: State Graphs & Agent SDKs
l-agentcore-strands.desc: Modeling agentic workflows as explicit state graphs (LangGraph, AWS Strands / Bedrock AgentCore) for robust, resumable multi-step execution.
